column7: Case Closed. Closed date : 2022-12-01 13:34:47.547 Case Resolved APCC direct response to const This email serves as a follow-up to your complaint that you logged with the Mayor's 24-Hr Hotline (311).  Since last year the Public Works Department (PWD) has changed the residential trash pickup hours to begin at 6am.  However  if it involves a business in a mixed residential/commercial zone  the state of Massachusetts does not give authority to municipalities and towns to regulate commercial trash pickup hours.  If that continues to be a problem to you  you may want to reach out to the Mayor's Office of Neighborhood Services (ONS) and the Back Bay Neighborhood Coordinator may address the issue with the business (see below).  I hope you find this information helpful.  Contact Information:  To reach a neighborhood liaison for Charlestown  email contactons@boston.gov or call 617-635-3485.     Sincerely   Lugardy Raymond

Case Closed. Closed date : Mon Apr 22 14:29:07 EDT 2024 Noted All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). For the BPRD Urban Forestry Division to consider removing a healthy public street tree for this reason  we need proof that the trees roots have caused the damage to the foundation of the home. Proof needs to be in the form of a written statement from a structural engineer stating the foundation cannot be repaired without removing the tree in question. Until the documentation is provided and the issue is investigated by the City Arborist the tree cannot be removed as it is protected by state law MGL CH 87. Proof should be sent to 1010 Massachusetts Ave Boston  MA 02118 3rd floor. Attn: Urban Forestry Division or emailed to (Trees@boston.gov) Attn: Urban Forestry Division.  There is a separate case (#2072239) open with the Public Works Department to repair the sidewalk at this location.

column7: Case Closed. Closed date : 2017-10-03 08:19:42.01 Case Resolved     Dear Constituent     National Grid is replacing a gas main that is almost 100 years old.  The city encourages this type of capital upgrade work.  It will provide dependable gas service to the residents as well as eliminate future emergency repairs.      The City acknowledges that the construction will cause an inconvenience to the residents.    However in the long run the placement of a new gas main is well worth it.     National Grid has to install the gas main first.  Once the main has been installed  then all service connections can be made.      If the City were to make National Grid pave to grade each day's trench work   it would cause multiple seams along the trench.    More importantly  every service connection needs to be cut into the trench.  If we were to pave the trench every day   this would result in 2 by 2 patches inside the trench area ending up with a very poor final paved product.     In order to have the best final paving  the City allows the utility company to leave the trench down low until all service connections are made and then pave the trench all at once  resulting in a smooth  level final trench repair.     Service laterals are topped within 24 hours of excavation.       What the constituent is observing is the trench left down low in preparation for one continuous final paving operation.    We do require that the contractor place florescent orange signs ahead of the construction informing motorists that the trench ahead is down low     For any questions or concerns  please contact the number listed below    Sincerely     Mark Cardarelli  Supervisor of Utility Coordination and Compliance  Boston Public Works Department  617-635-4951 Office
